1	import { useMemo } from "react";
2	import { getSaturation } from "../../lib/palette";
3	import { usePaletteStore } from "../../store/paletteStore";
4	import type { ColorEntry } from "../../types/palette";
5	import { W, H, PAD, PLOT_W, PLOT_H, toSvgX, toSvgY } from "./chartConstants";
6	
7	interface SaturationCurveProps {
8	  entries: ColorEntry[];
9	  showDots: boolean;
10	}
11	
12	export function SaturationCurve({ entries, showDots }: SaturationCurveProps) {
13	  const sMod = usePaletteStore((s) => s.sMod);
14	  const saturation = usePaletteStore((s) => s.saturation);
15	  const satMode = usePaletteStore((s) => s.satMode);
16	
17	  const { curvePath, yMin, yMax, effectiveDots, gridLines, gridTop } = useMemo(() => {
18	    // Sample the curve at every integer lightness
19	    const points: { x: number; y: number }[] = [];
20	    for (let l = 0; l <= 100; l++) {
21	      points.push({ x: l, y: getSaturation(l, sMod, satMode) });
22	    }
23	
24	    const allY = points.map((p) => p.y);
25	    const yMin = Math.min(...allY, 0);
26	    const rawMax = Math.max(...allY);
27	    // Snap to next 0.25 step so the chart fills the plot area cleanly
28	    const yMax = Math.ceil(rawMax / 0.25) * 0.25;
29	
30	    // Build SVG path for the multiplier curve
31	    const pathParts = points.map((p, i) => {
32	      const cmd = i === 0 ? "M" : "L";
33	      return `${cmd}${toSvgX(p.x).toFixed(2)},${toSvgY(p.y, yMin, yMax).toFixed(2)}`;
34	    });
35	
36	    // Dots for each palette entry showing effective saturation
37	    const effectiveDots = entries.map((e) => {
38	      const sK = getSaturation(e.lightness, sMod, satMode);
39	      const effective = saturation * sK;
40	      return {
41	        cx: toSvgX(e.lightness),
42	        cy: toSvgY(sK, yMin, yMax),
43	        effective: Math.round(effective * 100),
44	        lightness: e.lightness,
45	        hex: e.hex,
46	      };
47	    });
48	
49	    // Horizontal grid lines at round values, always including 1.0
50	    const step = yMax - yMin > 1.5 ? 0.5 : 0.25;
51	    const gridVals: number[] = [];
52	    const lo = Math.ceil(yMin / step) * step;
53	    for (let v = lo; v <= yMax; v += step) {
54	      gridVals.push(parseFloat(v.toFixed(2)));
55	    }
56	    if (!gridVals.includes(1)) gridVals.push(1);
57	    const gridLines = gridVals
58	      .sort((a, b) => a - b)
59	      .map((v) => ({
60	        y: toSvgY(v, yMin, yMax),
61	        label: v.toFixed(2),
62	      }));
63	
64	    const gridTop = gridLines.length > 0 ? Math.min(...gridLines.map((g) => g.y)) : PAD.top;
65	
66	    return { curvePath: pathParts.join(""), yMin, yMax, effectiveDots, gridLines, gridTop };
67	  }, [sMod, saturation, satMode, entries]);
68	
69	  // Vertical grid lines at lightness landmarks
70	  const xGridValues = [0, 25, 50, 75, 100];
71	
72	  return (
73	    <div>
74	      <svg
75	        viewBox={`0 0 ${W} ${H}`}
76	        className="w-full h-auto"
77	        role="img"
78	        aria-labelledby="sat-curve-title"
79	      >
80	        <title id="sat-curve-title">Saturation adjustment curve</title>
81	        {/* Grid lines - horizontal */}
82	        {gridLines.map((g) => (
83	          <g key={g.label}>
84	            <line
85	              x1={PAD.left}
86	              y1={g.y}
87	              x2={W - PAD.right}
88	              y2={g.y}
89	              stroke="var(--color-stroke-grid)"
90	              strokeWidth={1}
91	            />
92	            <text
93	              x={PAD.left - 8}
94	              y={g.y + 3}
95	              textAnchor="end"
96	              className="text-[13px] fill-text-faint font-mono"
97	            >
98	              {g.label}
99	            </text>
100	          </g>
101	        ))}
102	
103	        {/* Grid lines - vertical */}
104	        {xGridValues.map((l) => (
105	          <g key={l}>
106	            <line
107	              x1={toSvgX(l)}
108	              y1={gridTop}
109	              x2={toSvgX(l)}
110	              y2={H - PAD.bottom}
111	              stroke="var(--color-stroke-grid)"
112	              strokeWidth={1}
113	            />
114	            <text
115	              x={toSvgX(l)}
116	              y={H - PAD.bottom + 16}
117	              textAnchor="middle"
118	              className="text-[13px] fill-text-faint font-mono"
119	            >
120	              {l}
121	            </text>
122	          </g>
123	        ))}
124	
125	        {/* Axis labels */}
126	        <text
127	          x={PAD.left + PLOT_W / 2}
128	          y={H - 4}
129	          textAnchor="middle"
130	          className="text-[10px] fill-text-faint font-sans"
131	        >
132	          Lightness
133	        </text>
134	        <text
135	          x={12}
136	          y={PAD.top + PLOT_H / 2}
137	          textAnchor="middle"
138	          className="text-[10px] fill-text-faint font-sans"
139	          transform={`rotate(-90, 12, ${PAD.top + PLOT_H / 2})`}
140	        >
141	          Multiplier
142	        </text>
143	
144	        {/* 1.0 baseline */}
145	        <line
146	          x1={PAD.left}
147	          y1={toSvgY(1, yMin, yMax)}
148	          x2={W - PAD.right}
149	          y2={toSvgY(1, yMin, yMax)}
150	          stroke="var(--color-stroke-axis)"
151	          strokeWidth={1}
152	          strokeDasharray="4 3"
153	        />
154	
155	        {/* Saturation curve */}
156	        <path
157	          d={curvePath}
158	          fill="none"
159	          stroke="var(--color-stroke-primary)"
160	          strokeWidth={2}
161	          strokeLinecap="round"
162	          strokeLinejoin="round"
163	        />
164	
165	        {/* Palette entry dots */}
166	        {showDots && effectiveDots.map((d) => (
167	          <circle
168	            key={d.lightness}
169	            cx={d.cx}
170	            cy={d.cy}
171	            r={3}
172	            fill={d.hex}
173	            stroke="var(--color-stroke-secondary)"
174	            strokeWidth={1}
175	          />
176	        ))}
177	      </svg>

179	    </div>

181	}

```text
1	import chroma from "chroma-js";
2	import type { ColorEntry, PaletteParams, SatMode } from "../types/palette";
3	
4	/**
5	 * Parabolic saturation curve centered at lightness 50%.
6	 * See: https://www.desmos.com/calculator/02ufrfsuzy
7	 *
8	 * In "endpoint" mode the multiplier is 1.0 at L0/L100 (slider = endpoint saturation).
9	 * In "target" mode the multiplier is 1.0 at L50 (slider = vertex/target saturation).
10	 */
11	export function getSaturation(lightness: number, sMod: number, satMode: SatMode): number {
12	  if (sMod === 0) return 1;
13	  const o = 50;
14	  const raw = 1 + (Math.pow(lightness - o, 2) / sMod - Math.pow(o, 2) / sMod) / 100;
15	
16	  if (satMode === "endpoint") return raw;
17	
18	  // Normalize so vertex (L50) = 1.0, endpoints > 1.0
19	  const vertex = 1 - (Math.pow(o, 2) / sMod) / 100;
20	  if (vertex <= 0) return raw;
21	  return raw / vertex;
22	}
23	
24	export function generateNeutrals(params: PaletteParams): ColorEntry[] {
25	  const { hue, saturation, sMod, satMode, lMin, lMax } = params;
26	
27	  const entries: ColorEntry[] = [];
28	
29	  for (let i = lMin; i <= lMax; i++) {
30	    const l = i / 100;
31	    const sK = getSaturation(i, sMod, satMode);
32	    const color = chroma.hsl(hue, saturation * sK, l);
33	
34	    const [h, s, lVal] = color.hsl();
35	    const hRound = Math.round(h) || 0;
36	    const sRound = Math.round(s * 10000) / 100;
37	    const lRound = Math.round(lVal * 10000) / 100;
38	
39	    entries.push({
40	      label: `grey-${i}`,
41	      lightness: i,
42	      hsl: [h, s, lVal],
43	      hslString: `${hRound}deg ${sRound}% ${lRound}%`,
44	      hex: color.hex(),
45	    });
46	  }
47	
48	  return entries.reverse();
49	}
50
```
</details></tool-use>

```typescript
import { useEffect } from "react";
import { usePaletteStore } from "../store/paletteStore";

function buildFaviconSvg(hue: number): string {
  const color = `hsl(${hue}, 60%, 60%)`;
  return `<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 32 32">
  <rect width="32" height="32" rx="6" fill="#111"/>
  <path d="M6 10 Q16 26 26 10" fill="none" stroke="${color}" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  <circle cx="6" cy="10" r="2.5" fill="${color}"/>
  <circle cx="26" cy="10" r="2.5" fill="${color}"/>
</svg>`;
}

export function useDynamicFavicon() {
  const hue = usePaletteStore((s) => s.hue);

  useEffect(() => {
    const svg = buildFaviconSvg(hue);
    const blob = new Blob([svg], { type: "image/svg+xml" });
    const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);

    let link = document.querySelector<HTMLLinkElement>('link[rel="icon"]');
    if (!link) {
      link = document.createElement("link");
      link.rel = "icon";
      document.head.appendChild(link);
    }
    link.type = "image/svg+xml";
    link.href = url;

    return () => URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
  }, [hue]);
}

```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:28:05Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:28:08Z)**_

Currently we only have the SVG favicon and the dynamic JS version. For full spec compliance, we're missing:

- **ICO fallback** (32x32/16x16) — for older browsers that don't support SVG favicons
- **Apple touch icon** (180x180 PNG) — for iOS home screen bookmarks
- **PWA icons** (192x192, 512x512 PNG) — for `web.manifest` / Android home screen
- **`web.manifest`** file — declares the app name + icons for PWA contexts

The SVG + dynamic version covers modern browsers well. The question is how far you want to go — do you want the full set, or just the essentials (SVG + ICO + Apple touch icon)?

```text
Now I have comprehensive information from multiple authoritative sources. Let me compile the definitive guide.

---

# Exhaustive Favicon & Web Icon Best Practices (2025-2026)

## Executive Summary

The favicon landscape has simplified considerably. The old approach of generating 20+ files is dead. The modern gold standard requires **5-6 icon files, 1 manifest file, and optionally 1 OG image** -- plus the correct HTML tags. Below is every detail.

---

## 1. THE FILES

### 1A. favicon.ico (Legacy Fallback)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | ICO |
| **Dimensions** | 32x32 (single image), or multi-resolution 16x16 + 32x32 |
| **Required?** | Yes -- safety net for legacy clients |
| **Used by** | Older browsers, RSS readers, crawlers that request `/favicon.ico` directly |
| **Placement** | Must be at site root: `https://yourdomain.com/favicon.ico` |

**Key detail**: Some tools (RSS readers, certain crawlers) hardcode a request to `/favicon.ico` at the root and ignore `<link>` tags entirely. Always place this file at the root. A single 32x32 image inside the ICO container is sufficient unless your icon becomes blurry at 16x16, in which case embed both 16x16 and 32x32 as separate layers in the ICO file.

**HTML tag**:
```html
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="32x32">
```

The `sizes="32x32"` attribute is important -- it fixes a Chrome bug where Chrome would prefer the ICO file over an SVG file.

**Note**: Do NOT use `rel="shortcut icon"`. The `shortcut` token was never a valid link relation. Use `rel="icon"`.

---

### 1B. SVG Favicon (Modern Browsers)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | SVG |
| **Dimensions** | Vector (no fixed pixel size; must have a square `viewBox`) |
| **Required?** | Highly recommended |
| **Used by** | Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Opera (Safari has partial/improving support) |
| **Browser support** | ~72% of all browsers as of 2025 |

This is the single most powerful favicon file. SVG favicons:
- Scale perfectly at any resolution or zoom level
- Are typically smaller in file size than equivalent PNGs for logo-style graphics
- **Support dark mode** via embedded CSS media queries
- Can use P3 wide-gamut colors via media queries

**Dark mode example** (embed directly in the SVG):
```xml
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 500 500">
  <style>
    @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
      .logo { fill: #f0f0f0 }
    }
  </style>
  <path class="logo" fill="#0f0f0f" d="..." />
</svg>
```

**HTML tag**:
```html
<link rel="icon" href="/icon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">
```

**Alternative approach for dark mode** (two separate files):
```html
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon-dark.svg" type="image/svg+xml" media="(prefers-color-scheme: dark)">
```

---

### 1C. Apple Touch Icon (iOS/iPadOS)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | PNG (no transparency -- iOS fills transparent areas with black) |
| **Dimensions** | 180x180 px |
| **Required?** | Yes |
| **Used by** | iOS Safari (Add to Home Screen), iPadOS, macOS Safari, Siri Suggestions, Spotlight search |

**Key details**:
- Only one size is needed: 180x180. iOS automatically downscales for all devices (iPhone, iPad, older models). The old practice of specifying multiple sizes (76x76, 120x120, 152x152) is obsolete.
- Add ~20px padding around the icon content and use a solid background color for best results. iOS auto-applies rounded corners and a subtle shadow.
- Avoid transparency -- iOS replaces transparent pixels with black.
- This icon is also used by Android as a fallback if no web manifest is present.

**HTML tag**:
```html
<link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">
```

**Placement note**: Some older iOS versions look for `/apple-touch-icon.png` at the site root even without a `<link>` tag. Place it at the root for maximum compatibility.

---

### 1D. PNG Fallback (48x48)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | PNG |
| **Dimensions** | 48x48 px |
| **Required?** | Recommended (some sources consider this the SVG fallback) |
| **Used by** | Safari (where SVG support is partial), Google Search results |

Google Search requires a favicon of **at least 48x48 pixels**, square, readable at small sizes, hosted on the same domain. A 48x48 PNG satisfies this and serves as the universal fallback for browsers that do not support SVG favicons.

**HTML tag**:
```html
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon-48x48.png" sizes="48x48" type="image/png">
```

---

### 1E. Web Manifest Icons (PWA / Android / Windows)

These are declared in the manifest file, not in HTML `<link>` tags.

#### icon-192.png

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | PNG |
| **Dimensions** | 192x192 px |
| **Purpose** | `any` (default) |
| **Required?** | Yes for PWA |
| **Used by** | Android home screen, Chrome "Add to Home Screen", Windows (via Edge) |

#### icon-512.png

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | PNG |
| **Dimensions** | 512x512 px |
| **Purpose** | `any` |
| **Required?** | Yes for PWA |
| **Used by** | PWA splash screen, Google Play Store (TWA), high-resolution displays |

#### icon-maskable-192.png

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | PNG |
| **Dimensions** | 192x192 px |
| **Purpose** | `maskable` |
| **Required?** | Recommended for polished Android experience |
| **Used by** | Android 8+ launchers (applies circle, teardrop, squircle masks) |

#### icon-maskable-512.png

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | PNG |
| **Dimensions** | 512x512 px |
| **Purpose** | `maskable` |
| **Required?** | Recommended |
| **Used by** | Chrome OS, macOS Sonoma+, Android 8+ |

**Maskable icon safe zone**: The visible area is a circle centered on the icon with radius = 40% of the icon size (i.e., a 409x409 circle within a 512x512 canvas). Content outside this circle may be clipped. Design the maskable icon with extra padding and a solid background. Test at [maskable.app](https://maskable.app/).

**Do NOT use `"purpose": "any maskable"`** on a single icon. This is widely discouraged because maskable icons have extra padding that looks wrong when displayed without a mask. Provide separate files for `any` and `maskable`.

---

### 1F. OG Image (Social Sharing)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Format** | PNG or JPEG (PNG for graphics/text, JPEG for photos) |
| **Dimensions** | 1200x630 px (universal sweet spot, 1.91:1 aspect ratio) |
| **File size** | Under 300KB ideal, max 8MB (Facebook limit) |
| **Required?** | Not a favicon per se, but essential for social sharing |
| **Used by** | Facebook, Twitter/X, LinkedIn, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Pinterest, iMessage, etc. |

Must be served over HTTPS at an absolute URL. Keep important content within the central 1080x566 area (safe zone).

---

## 2. THE MANIFEST FILE

**Filename**: `manifest.webmanifest` (or `site.webmanifest`, or `manifest.json` -- all work, `.webmanifest` is the spec-correct extension)

```json
{
  "name": "Chroma Curve",
  "short_name": "ChromaCurve",
  "description": "Monochromatic palette generator with parabolic saturation curve",
  "start_url": "/",
  "display": "standalone",
  "background_color": "#0a0a0a",
  "theme_color": "#0a0a0a",
  "icons": [
    {
      "src": "/icon-192.png",
      "type": "image/png",
      "sizes": "192x192",
      "purpose": "any"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icon-maskable-192.png",
      "type": "image/png",
      "sizes": "192x192",
      "purpose": "maskable"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icon-512.png",
      "type": "image/png",
      "sizes": "512x512",
      "purpose": "any"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icon-maskable-512.png",
      "type": "image/png",
      "sizes": "512x512",
      "purpose": "maskable"
    }
  ]
}
```

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `name` | Full app name (shown in install prompts, system menus) |
| `short_name` | Abbreviated name under home screen icon (max 10-12 chars) |
| `start_url` | URL opened when launching from home screen |
| `display` | `standalone` (app-like), `fullscreen`, `minimal-ui`, or `browser` |
| `background_color` | Splash screen background during PWA load |
| `theme_color` | Android status bar color, system UI tinting |

---

## 3. ALL HTML TAGS (Complete `<head>` Block)

```html
<!-- Favicons -->
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="32x32">
<link rel="icon" href="/icon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon-48x48.png" sizes="48x48" type="image/png">
<link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">

<!-- Web App Manifest -->
<link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.webmanifest">

<!-- Theme Color (with light/dark variants) -->
<meta name="theme-color" content="#0a0a0a" media="(prefers-color-scheme: dark)">
<meta name="theme-color" content="#ffffff" media="(prefers-color-scheme: light)">

<!-- Color Scheme (tells browser to style its own UI elements correctly) -->
<meta name="color-scheme" content="dark light">

<!-- Open Graph -->
<meta property="og:type" content="website">
<meta property="og:url" content="https://chromacurve.style">
<meta property="og:title" content="Chroma Curve">
<meta property="og:description" content="Monochromatic palette generator with parabolic saturation curve">
<meta property="og:image" content="https://chromacurve.style/og-image.png">
<meta property="og:image:width" content="1200">
<meta property="og:image:height" content="630">
<meta property="og:image:alt" content="Chroma Curve palette generator preview">
<meta property="og:site_name" content="Chroma Curve">

<!-- Twitter Card -->
<meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image">
<meta name="twitter:title" content="Chroma Curve">
<meta name="twitter:description" content="Monochromatic palette generator with parabolic saturation curve">
<meta name="twitter:image" content="https://chromacurve.style/og-image.png">
<meta name="twitter:image:alt" content="Chroma Curve palette generator preview">
```

---

## 4. THEME-COLOR META TAG

The `theme-color` meta tag tints the browser chrome (address bar on Android Chrome, status bar on iOS Safari 15+). You can specify separate values for light and dark modes using the `media` attribute:

```html
<meta name="theme-color" content="#0a0a0a" media="(prefers-color-scheme: dark)">
<meta name="theme-color" content="#ffffff" media="(prefers-color-scheme: light)">
```

**Browser support**:
- Chrome (Android): Full support, colors the address bar
- Safari 15+ (iOS): Supports `media` attribute for light/dark switching; colors the status bar
- Chrome (desktop, PWA only): Supported for installed PWAs starting Chrome 93
- Edge: Supported

**Important**: If your site has a theme toggle (not just OS-level preference), you must also update the `<meta name="theme-color">` tag via JavaScript when the user toggles, in addition to setting CSS `color-scheme`. Otherwise the browser chrome stays stale until page reload.

Also declare `color-scheme` so the browser draws its own UI elements (scrollbars, form controls) correctly:

```html
<meta name="color-scheme" content="dark light">
```

The manifest file also has a `theme_color` field -- this is used specifically for installed PWAs.

---

## 5. DEPRECATED / NO LONGER NEEDED

### Safari Pinned Tab Icon (`mask-icon`)
**Status**: Obsolete since Safari 12. Safari now uses the regular favicon for pinned tabs. Even apple.com no longer uses it. Skip it.

### Windows Tile / browserconfig.xml
**Status**: Legacy. Was for Windows 8/8.1 Start Screen tiles and Internet Explorer 11. Windows 8 lost Microsoft support on January 10, 2023. Modern Edge uses the web manifest. Skip it unless you specifically target legacy Windows environments.

The old tile sizes were: 70x70 (126x126 recommended), 150x150 (270x270), 310x150 (558x270), 310x310 (558x558). These are no longer needed.

### `msapplication-TileColor` and `msapplication-TileImage` meta tags
**Status**: Legacy IE/Edge. No longer needed for modern Edge (which is Chromium-based).

### Opera Coast (228x228)
**Status**: Dead. The browser left the App Store in 2017.

### `rel="shortcut icon"`
**Status**: `shortcut` was never a valid link relation. Always use `rel="icon"`.

### Multiple Apple Touch Icon sizes
**Status**: Unnecessary. A single 180x180 is sufficient. iOS downscales automatically.

---

## 6. PLATFORM-SPECIFIC NOTES

### Samsung Internet
Uses the standard web manifest and `<link rel="icon">` tags. No proprietary requirements. Samsung Internet is Chromium-based and follows the same conventions as Chrome.

### Opera (Desktop/Mobile)
Supports SVG favicons. May choose from available icons at random rather than by priority. No special requirements beyond the standard set.

### UC Browser
Chromium-based. Uses standard favicon and manifest conventions. No proprietary requirements.

### Firefox
Full SVG favicon support. Uses `<link rel="icon">` tags. Supports web manifest for PWA.

### Google Search Results
Requires a favicon of at least **48x48 pixels**. Must be declared with `<link rel="icon">`. Must be hosted on the same domain as the indexed page. Accepts ICO, SVG, and PNG. Google may choose to not display your favicon if it deems it inappropriate.

---

## 7. COMPLETE FILE CHECKLIST

| File | Dimensions | Format | Priority | Declared In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `favicon.ico` | 32x32 (or 16+32 multi) | ICO | Required | HTML `<link>` + site root |
| `icon.svg` | Vector (square viewBox) | SVG | Highly recommended | HTML `<link>` |
| `favicon-48x48.png` | 48x48 | PNG | Recommended | HTML `<link>` |
| `apple-touch-icon.png` | 180x180 | PNG (opaque) | Required | HTML `<link>` + site root |
| `icon-192.png` | 192x192 | PNG | Required (PWA) | Manifest |
| `icon-512.png` | 512x512 | PNG | Required (PWA) | Manifest |
| `icon-maskable-192.png` | 192x192 | PNG | Recommended | Manifest |
| `icon-maskable-512.png` | 512x512 | PNG | Recommended | Manifest |
| `manifest.webmanifest` | N/A | JSON | Required (PWA) | HTML `<link>` |
| `og-image.png` | 1200x630 | PNG/JPEG | Recommended | HTML `<meta>` |

**Total**: 8-9 image files + 1 JSON manifest + 1 OG image = 10-11 files for the gold standard.

---

## 8. PRODUCTION WORKFLOW

1. Start with a high-quality square SVG of your logo
2. Add dark mode CSS media query inside the SVG
3. Export the SVG as `icon.svg`
4. Generate 32x32 ICO from the SVG (via ImageMagick or GIMP)
5. Export PNGs at 48, 180, 192, 512 from the SVG
6. Create maskable variants at 192 and 512 (center content within 40% radius safe zone, add solid background)
7. For the apple-touch-icon, add ~20px padding and a solid background
8. Optimize all PNGs with OxiPNG/Squoosh (target 64-color reduced palette)
9. Optimize SVG with SVGO (`npx svgo --multipass icon.svg`)
10. Create the manifest.webmanifest
11. Add all HTML tags
12. Design a 1200x630 OG image for social sharing
13. Test with [RealFaviconGenerator checker](https://realfavicongenerator.net/), Facebook Sharing Debugger, and Twitter Card Validator

---

## **Favicon Best Practices 2025-2026: Complete Guide**

### **Essential Sizes**

Modern favicon implementation requires multiple sizes including 16x16px for browser tabs and bookmarks, and 512x512px for PWA icons and app store listings. At minimum, include a 32x32 PNG, 180x180 Apple Touch Icon, and a manifest.json with 192x192 and 512x512 icons.

### **Format Recommendations**

A simple combination of an .svg for scalability and a 48×48 .png fallback is now sufficient to cover almost all use cases while reducing code complexity and maintenance. Provide both ICO and PNG formats for maximum compatibility, and use SVG for modern browsers that support it.

### **Design Best Practices**

Simplicity is key when designing favicons—at 16x16 pixels, complex designs become unreadable blurs, so use simple, recognizable designs, avoid complex details, stick to 2-3 colors maximum, and ensure high contrast. Test legibility by viewing your favicon at actual size before finalizing.

### **Modern Implementation**

Use SVG favicons for modern browsers since they remain crisp at any zoom level and support Dark Mode media queries directly within the file. Instead of serving dozens of icons, all you need is just five icons and one JSON file.

### **Professional Benefits**

Websites without favicons or with default browser icons appear incomplete and unprofessional, whereas a custom favicon demonstrates that you care about every aspect of your website's user experience.

## Essential Favicon Files for 2025

Modern websites need just five icons and one JSON file, with essential files including `/favicon.ico`, an SVG icon, and an apple-touch-icon.

### Key File Types:

1. **SVG Favicon**: The PNG and SVG favicons are the most important ones nowadays, appearing in browser tabs and Google result pages. Modern browsers support SVG favicons with CSS media queries for dark mode.

2. **PNG & ICO Files**: Best practices include shipping PNG (16×16, 32×32) and ICO for broad favicon compatibility, along with SVG favicon for modern browsers plus PNG fallbacks.

3. **Apple Touch Icon**: The Apple Touch icon is a must have because it serves as the high resolution icon, used by iOS and Android if there is no web app manifest, as a shortcut icon on the home screen of mobile devices.

## Web Manifest Configuration

The web app manifest (site.webmanifest) is a JSON file that tells browsers how to display your site when installed as a PWA, including all required fields like name, short_name, icons, start_url, and display.

## HTML Head Setup

Modern favicon HTML includes favicon.ico as a fallback for legacy browsers, a PNG for browser UI and Google SERPs, SVG for modern browsers, apple-touch-icon for iOS, and a web manifest for PWAs.

## Dark Mode Support

SVG favicons scale infinitely and support features like dark mode through CSS media queries.

The search results confirm that in 2025, favicon setup has evolved beyond the single .ico file to support multiple formats and platforms while remaining relatively minimal in scope.

<!-- Source: https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/how-to-favicon-in-2021-six-files-that-fit-most-needs -->
# How to Favicon in 2026: Three files that fit most needs—Martian Chronicles, Evil Martians’ team blog
*By Andrey Sitnik*
---
It’s time to rethink how we cook a set of favicons for modern browsers and stop the icon generator madness. Frontend developers currently have to deal with 20+ static PNG files just to display a tiny website logo in a browser tab or on a touchscreen. Read on to see how to take a smarter approach and adopt a minimal set of icons that fits most modern needs.
[
![Book a call](https://evilmartians.com/static/ira-a6db0f09d8c14693c5f73dcff15b70fa.webp)
**Irina Nazarova** CEO at Evil Martians
Book a call
](https://cal.com/team/evilmartians/exploration)
The topic of favicons has proven to be more exhaustive than anyone could’ve ever wished, so I’ve also summarized the entire article in just two code snippets for those who’ve already suffered enough and who know exactly what to do. Still, I recommend geeking out to the rest of it!
## The extremely short version
Instead of serving dozens of icons, all you need is just **five** icons and one JSON file.
For the browser using HTML:
```
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="32x32">
<link rel="icon" href="/icon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">
<link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png"><!-- 180×180 -->
```
If you’re making a PWA (Progressive Web App), also add this to the HTML:
```
<link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.webmanifest">
```
And a file with the web app manifest:
```
// manifest.webmanifest
{
  "icons": [
    { "src": "/icon-192.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "192x192" },
    { "src": "/icon-mask.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "512x512", "purpose": "maskable" },
    { "src": "/icon-512.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "512x512" }
  ]
}
```
Maskable icons should have [bigger paddings](https://w3c.github.io/manifest/#icon-masks). The safe zone is a 409×409 circle. Use [maskable.app](https://maskable.app/) to check your icon.
That’s it. If you want to know how I got here, the compromises I had to make, and how to build a set like this from scratch in a step-by-step fashion, stay tuned in for the rest of the article.
## The long version, where everything is explained
> Perfection is achieved, not when there is nothing more to add, but when there is nothing left to take away.
The concept of a favicon, which is short for “favorite icon”, have been around since the early 2000s. We’ve all seen those cute little images in our browser’s tab bar which help us differentiate our open websites. Users _expect_ your website to have a favicon. It’s one of those little things that make other people take you seriously.
Even Apple, which has always had some kind of aesthetic beef with icons that don’t come from Cupertino, downplaying favicons in Safari for years, has finally given up and now properly displays them across all of its devices.
> If you have a public-facing website, it has to have a favicon. Sadly, what users perceive as one icon is actually _a lot_ of them.
So, it’s common to offload the grueling task of generating these necessary files for an ever-growing list of screens and devices to favicon generator tools. No one in their right mind would ever want to spend hours creating them by hand. We’re here to build websites, after all, not to make browser vendors happy.
![A set of favicons generated by a popular online generator](https://evilmartians.com/static/e1f0bbd363aeb610e0e12e7687d1f272/b6494/generated.png)
A set of favicons generated by a popular online generator
*A set of favicons generated by a popular online generator*
As a creator of [NanoID](https://evilmartians.com/opensource/nanoid/) and a proponent of minimalistic open source, I tend to think in a slightly different direction. What is the most efficient set of website icons? Which formats are outdated? Which icon types can be replaced with small compromises?
Thus, I set out to create a minimal list of favicons that will work in all cases and in all browsers—barring some edge cases—and even then, this will still work, just not 100% perfectly.
## The Ultimate Favicon Setup
Instead of creating many images with different sizes, I decided to rely on SVG and browser downscaling. If you’re concerned about performance, I’m here to set the record straight:
-   Browsers download favicons in the background, so a bigger favicon image does not affect website performance.
-   SVG is a great way to reduce image size for images that aren’t supposed to be bitmaps in the first place; for many _logos_ the resulting file will be much smaller than a PNG.
-   With just three PNG images in this minimum set, you can use advanced tools to optimize their size. This solves a problem for Internet users that don’t have unlimited data plans.
Now I’ll reveal the minimal set of icons that I came up with during my research and practice. This list should work with all popular browsers and devices, both old and new.
#### I. favicon.ico for legacy browsers
ICO files actually have a [directory structure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ICO_\(file_format\)#Icon_resource_structure) and can pack files with different resolutions. I recommend sticking to a single 32×32 image, unless the one you have doesn’t downscale well to 16×16 (if it becomes blurry, for instance). In that case, you can ask your designer to come up with a special version of the logo that’s tailored to fit small pixel grids.
Don’t get smart with the folder static asset folder structure and cache busters. `https://example.com` website should have a favicon on `https://example.com/favicon.ico`. Some tools, like RSS readers, just request `/favicon.ico` from the server and don’t bother looking elsewhere.
We need `sizes="32x32"` for `<link>` to `.ico` file in order to fix the [Chrome bug](https://twitter.com/subzey/status/1417099064949235712) where it chooses an ICO file over an SVG.
#### II. A single SVG icon with a light/dark version for modern browsers
SVG is a vector format that describes curves instead of pixels. At large sizes, it’s more efficient than raster images. As of this writing, [72% of all browsers](https://caniuse.com/link-icon-svg) support SVG icons.
Your HTML page should have a `<link>` tag in its `<head>` with `rel="icon"`, `type="image/svg+xml"` and with the `href` containing a link to the SVG file as attributes.
SVG is an XML format and can contain a `<style>` tag to describes CSS. As with any CSS, it can contain media queries like `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)`. This will allow you to toggle the same icon between [light and dark system themes](https://web.dev/building-an-adaptive-favicon/).
#### III. 180×180 PNG image for Apple devices
The Apple touch icon is an image that Apple devices will use if you add the webpage as a shortcut to your home screen on an iPhone or iPad. Your HTML page should have `<link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="apple-touch-icon.png">` tag inside `<head>`.
Since iOS 8+, iPads have required an image with a 180×180 resolution. Other devices will downscale it, but if we provide the source with a high-enough quality, the downscaling won’t hurt the end-user (I’ll come back to this later).
Small note: an Apple touch icon will look better if you place `20px` padding around the icon and add some background color. You can use any image editor to do this.
#### IV. Web app manifest with 192×192 and 512×512 PNG icons for Android devices
-   A web app manifest is a JSON file containing all the details for a browser to install your website as a system application. This format came about from Google via its [PWA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Progressive_web_application) initiative.
-   Your HTML page should have a `<link rel="manifest" href="path.webmanifest">` tag with a link to the manifest file.
-   The manifest should have an `icon` field that links to three icons: 192×192, for display on the home screen, a 512×512 maskable icon for different Android launchers, and a 512×512 which will be used as a splash screen while the PWA is loading.
-   The maskable icon should have [bigger paddings](https://w3c.github.io/manifest/#icon-masks) around the icon so it can be cropped by the launcher to fit its design. The safe zone is a central circle of 409×409. You can use [maskable.app](https://maskable.app/) to check your icon.
```
{
  "icons": [
    {
      "src": "/icon-192.png",
      "type": "image/png",
      "sizes": "192x192"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icon-mask.png",
      "type": "image/png",
      "sizes": "512x512",
      "purpose": "maskable"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icon-512.png",
      "type": "image/png",
      "sizes": "512x512"
    }
  ]
}
```
## Did we forget anyone?
There are, of course, more favicon flavors out there, some of them quite obscure, so let’s see how our setup fares with them. Maybe, it’s time to say farewell to some of the less successful formats out there.
#### Windows Tile Icon
Microsoft Edge used to [support](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/windows/internet-explorer/ie-developer/platform-apis/hh772707\(v=vs.85\)) a special icon format to pin websites to the start menu. For recent versions of Windows, this is no longer required.
#### Safari Pinned Icon
Safari formerly had a requirement of SVG monochrome icon for [pinned tabs](https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/AppleApplications/Reference/SafariWebContent/pinnedTabs/pinnedTabs.html). However, since Safari 12, we can use a regular favicon for pinned tabs. Even [apple.com](https://www.apple.com/) doesn’t use the `mask-icon` anymore.
#### rel=“shortcut”
A lot of (now outdated) tutorials will include a `favicon.ico` into HTML like this:
```
<link rel="shortcut icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="32x32">
```
Be warned that `shortcut` is not, and never has been, a valid link relation. Read this [amazing article](https://mathiasbynens.be/notes/rel-shortcut-icon) by Mathias Bynens from ten years ago that explains why we never needed shortcuts and why `rel="icon"` is just fine.
#### Opera Coast
In the past, Opera Coast, an experimental browser for iOS, required a special 228×228 icon. This browser left the App Store in 2017, and I doubt it survived the multiple iOS updates since that time.
Now, as we wave good-bye to our fallen comrades, let’s see how to produce an ultimate favicon set for those who are still standing.
## How to build our Ultimate Favicon Set
Here’s how to build our ultimate, minimalistic favicon set in six quick steps. All you need to start is an SVG file for the logo that you want to use.
#### Step 1: Prepare the SVG
Be sure that the SVG image is _square_. Open the source file in your system viewer and check the image’s width and height. It’s easy to adjust the SVG size using any SVG editor. In [Inkscape](https://inkscape.org/), you can change document size by selecting File → Document Properties and then center the logo using Object → Align and Distribute.
Save your file as `icon.svg`. Now let’s fiddle with our SVG and make it play well with modern system _themes_. Ask your designer how the colors should be inverted for a dark theme (for B&W logos, you just change black to white).
Now, open your SVG file in a _text_ editor. Find a `<path>` with a dark or missing `fill`. Add a CSS media query that will trigger on theme changes and change `fill` to the colors you want:
```
  <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 500 500">
+   <style>
+     @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
+     .a { fill: #f0f0f0 }
+     }
+   </style>
-   <path fill="#0f0f0f" d="…" />
+   <path class="a" fill="#0f0f0f" d="…" />
  </svg>
```
You may also use this media query technique with SVGs to [add wide-gamut P3 colors](https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/how-to-use-p3-colors-in-svg) to your favicons.
#### Step 2: Create an ICO file
Open your `icon.svg` file in a raster graphics editor. I recommend [GIMP](https://www.gimp.org/); it’s free and multi-platform.
Accept rendering SVG to raster. Set the width and height to be 32 pixels. Export file to `favicon.ico` using _32 bpp, 8-bit alpha, no palette_ settings.
If you do not have GIMP you can install [Inkscape](https://inkscape.org/) and [ImageMagick](https://www.imagemagick.org/script/download.php) and convert SVG to ICO in the terminal:
```
inkscape ./icon.svg --export-width=32 --export-filename="./tmp.png"
# In Windows call `magick convert ./tmp.png ./favicon.ico`
convert ./tmp.png ./favicon.ico
rm ./tmp.png
```
Scale the image down to 16×16 and check the icon visibility. If it has become too blurry, it would be better to ask your designer for a custom tiny version of the logo.
To include a separate 16×16 version of an icon:
1.  Open `favicon.ico` with the 32×32 icon.
2.  Create a new layer with a 16×16 size.
3.  Put the 16×16 version of an icon into this layer.
4.  Export the file. GIMP will save each layout as a separate version of the icon.
Or you can do the same in ImageMagick by:
```
convert ./icon-32.png ./icon-16.png ./favicon.ico
```
#### Step 3: Create PNG images
Open your source SVG file in a raster graphics editor again and create a 512×512 image. Export it as `icon-512.png`.
Scale the image to 192×192 and export it to `icon-192.png`. Now scale the image itself to 140×140 and set the canvas to 180×180, and then export it as `apple-touch-icon.png`.
Then, scale it to 409×409 and increase the canvas to 512×512, and export it as `icon-mask.png`. Check the icon in the [maskable.app](https://maskable.app/) with different masks and tune the icon size if needed.
Or you can do the same in Inkscape:
```
inkscape --export-type="png" --export-width=512 --export-filename="./icon-512.png" ./icon.svg
inkscape --export-type="png" --export-width=192 --export-filename="./icon-192.png" ./icon.svg
```
#### Step 4: Optimize PNG and SVG files
The best tool for optimizing SVGs is [SVGO](https://github.com/svg/svgo). To use it, run:
```
npx svgo --multipass icon.svg
```
[Squoosh](https://squoosh.app/) is a great web app to optimize raster images:
1.  Open your `icon-512.png` in Squoosh.
2.  Change the Compress setting to `OxiPNG`.
3.  Enable “Reduce palette”.
4.  Set 64 colors.
5.  Compare the before/after by moving the slider. If you see a difference, increase the number of colors.
6.  Save the file.
Repeat these steps for `icon-192.png` and `apple-touch-icon.png`.
#### Step 5: Add the icons to HTML
You need to add links to `favicon.ico` and to `apple-touch-icon.png` into your HTML.
For static HTML:
```
  <title>My website</title>
+ <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="32x32">
+ <link rel="icon" href="/icon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">
+ <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">
```
However, we recommend using a bundler to generate cache busters (include the file’s hash in the name as a fingerprint). If you are using Webpack with [`HtmlWebpackPlugin`](https://webpack.js.org/plugins/html-webpack-plugin/):
1.  Create an `index.html` template.
2.  Add the template to the plugin options:
    ```
    new HtmlWebpackPlugin({ template: "./view/index.html" });
    ```
3.  Define an HTML template with links (the examples use `HtmlWebpackPlugin` to include files, but it can be your templating language of choice):
    ```
    <!DOCTYPE html>
    <html lang="en">
      <head>
        <meta charset="utf-8">
        <title>My website</title>
        <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1">
        <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="32x32">
        <link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="<%=
          require('./icon.svg').default
        %>">
        <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="<%=
          require('./apple-touch-icon.png').default
        %>"
       >
      </head>
      <body></body>
    </html>
    ```
4.  Use [`copy-webpack-plugin`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/copy-webpack-plugin) to copy `favicon.ico` _without_ adding a hash to file name.
#### Bonus tip: create a separate icon for staging
Favicons are a nice way to distinguish your production environment from a staging one. I find that using an alternative icon for staging is super effective for avoiding any costly cases of confusion.
Create a `favicon-dev.ico` with the same logo, but invert the colors (or do something that makes sense to you). Do the same for SVG and create `icon-dev.svg`.
Now, replace the icons in your HTML template depending on the `process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'` condition:
```
  <!doctype html>
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charset="utf-8">
      <title>My website</title>
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1">
-     <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="32x32">
+     <link rel="icon" sizes="32x32" href="<%=
+       process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'
+         ? '/favicon.ico'
+         : require('./favicon-dev.ico').default
+     %>">
      <link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="<%=
-       require('./icon.svg').default
+       process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'
+         ? require('./icon.svg').default
+         : require('./icon-dev.svg').default
      %>">
      <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="<%=
        require('./apple-touch-icon.png').default
      %>">
    </head>
    <body></body>
  </html>
```
#### Step 6: Create a web manifest
For static HTML, create a JSON file named `manifest.webmanifest`:
```
{
  "name": "My website",
  "icons": [
    { "src": "/icon-192.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "192x192" },
    { "src": "/icon-mask.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "512x512", "purpose": "maskable" },
    { "src": "/icon-512.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "512x512" }
  ]
}
```
Link it in your HTML:
```
  <title>My website</title>
+ <link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.webmanifest">
  <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" sizes="32x32">
  <link rel="icon" href="/icon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">
  <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">
```
With Webpack, you can use [webpack-pwa-manifest](https://www.npmjs.com/package/webpack-pwa-manifest) plugin:
```
  plugins: [
    …,
    new WebpackPwaManifest({
      name: 'My website',
      icons: [
        { src: resolve('./icon-192.png'), sizes: '192x192' },
        { src: resolve('./icon-mask.png'), sizes: '512x512', purpose: 'maskable' },
        { src: resolve('./icon-512.png'), sizes: '512x512' }
      ]
    })
  ]
```
Thank you for reading! As you can see, with modern web standards, the task of creating our Ultimate Favicon Set becomes quite straightforward. And even though following the steps manually shouldn’t take much of your time, having an automated tool to do the same will make things even more amazing! Feel free to ping me on [Twitter](https://twitter.com/sitnikcode) if you’re willing to build one; I’ll be more than happy to help!
Changelog
### 2024-09-05
-   Added maskable icon for PWA.
### 2023-11-19
-   Fixed CLI commands for ICO and PNG generation.
### 2023-07-11
-   `sizes="any"` for ICO file was replaced to `sizes="32x32"` to fix bug when Chrome downloaded both ICO and SVG files.
### 2021-07-19
-   `sizes="any"` was added to ICO file to fix bug when Chrome took ICO file instead of SVG.
### 2021-01-19
-   Inkscape commands were added to convert SVG to PNG.
### 2021-01-15
-   ImageMagick commands were added for users who do not have GIMP.
```

<!-- Source: https://favicon.im/blog/complete-favicon-size-format-guide-2025 -->
# Complete Favicon Size and Format Guide 2025: From 16x16 to 512x512 Pixels
The favicon landscape has evolved dramatically from simple 16x16 pixel ICO files to sophisticated multi-platform icon systems. Modern websites require up to 15 different favicon sizes and formats to ensure perfect display across browsers, mobile devices, and Progressive Web Apps.
This comprehensive guide provides everything you need to create a professional favicon system in 2025. Whether you're building a simple blog or a complex web application, you'll learn which sizes matter most, when to use specific formats, and how to optimize for performance without compromising quality.
**What You'll Learn:**
-   Essential favicon sizes for maximum compatibility
-   Format selection strategies (ICO vs PNG vs SVG)
-   Platform-specific implementation requirements
-   Performance optimization techniques
-   Common issues and professional solutions
## Standard Favicon Sizes Breakdown
### Browser Standard Sizes
**16x16 pixels - Classic Browser Tab Icon**
-   **Primary Use:** Browser tabs, address bar, bookmarks
-   **Format:** ICO, PNG
-   **Importance:** ★★★★★ (Critical)
-   **Technical Note:** This is the fundamental size that all websites must provide for basic functionality
-   **SEO Impact:** Improves bookmark recognition and tab identification
**32x32 pixels - High-Resolution Browser Support**
-   **Primary Use:** Browser tabs on high-resolution displays (Retina, 4K)
-   **Format:** ICO, PNG
-   **Importance:** ★★★★★ (Critical)
-   **Technical Note:** Modern browsers' preferred size for crystal-clear display
-   **Performance:** Minimal impact while providing significant visual improvement
**48x48 pixels - Windows Desktop Shortcuts**
-   **Primary Use:** Windows desktop shortcut icons, taskbar
-   **Format:** ICO, PNG
-   **Importance:** ★★★★☆ (High)
-   **Technical Note:** Activated when users create desktop shortcuts to your website
-   **User Experience:** Enhances professional appearance in Windows environments
### Mobile Device Specific Sizes
**152x152 pixels - iOS Safari Bookmarks & iPad**
-   **Primary Use:** iOS Safari bookmarks, iPad home screen shortcuts
-   **Format:** PNG (no transparency)
-   **Importance:** ★★★★☆ (High)
-   **Technical Requirement:** Transparent backgrounds automatically filled with white
-   **Device Coverage:** Older iPad models and iOS Safari bookmark system
**180x180 pixels - iOS Home Screen Icon**
-   **Primary Use:** iPhone home screen "Add to Home Screen" feature
-   **Format:** PNG with solid background
-   **Importance:** ★★★★★ (Critical)
-   **Technical Requirement:** iOS automatically applies rounded corner effects and shadow
-   **User Impact:** Essential for mobile web app functionality and brand recognition
**192x192 pixels - Android Home Screen**
-   **Primary Use:** Android device home screen icons, Chrome "Add to Home Screen"
-   **Format:** PNG with transparency support
-   **Importance:** ★★★★★ (Critical)
-   **PWA Requirement:** Minimum size requirement for Progressive Web App applications
-   **Brand Consistency:** Ensures professional appearance across all Android devices
### PWA and Modern Application Sizes
**512x512 pixels - PWA Application Icon**
-   **Primary Use:** PWA splash screens, app store submissions, high-resolution displays
-   **Format:** PNG with transparency
-   **Importance:** ★★★★★ (Critical for PWAs)
-   **Technical Requirement:** Required size for PWA manifest files and Google Play Store
-   **Performance Impact:** Larger file size but essential for app-like functionality
-   **Quality Standards:** Must be crisp and readable when scaled down
**1024x1024 pixels - Future-Proof High-Resolution**
-   **Primary Use:** Future device compatibility, ultra-high-resolution displays
-   **Format:** PNG with transparency
-   **Importance:** ★★★☆☆ (Optional but recommended)
-   **Strategic Value:** Prepares for next-generation display technology
-   **File Size Consideration:** Optimize heavily to maintain performance
## File Format Selection Guide
### ICO Format - Traditional and Widely Supported
**Advantages:**
-   Perfect support across all browsers
-   Can contain multiple sizes in a single file
-   Native Windows system support
**Disadvantages:**
-   Relatively larger file size
-   No support for modern compression algorithms
-   Fewer creation and editing tools available
**Recommended Use Cases:**
-   Traditional website compatibility requirements
-   Windows desktop applications
-   Need for single-file multiple-size solutions
### PNG Format - Modern Browser Preferred
**Advantages:**
-   Excellent compression ratio and image quality
-   Perfect transparency support
-   Wide tool support
**Disadvantages:**
-   Need to create separate files for each size
-   Limited support in some older browsers
**Recommended Use Cases:**
-   Modern web applications
-   Mobile device optimization
-   Designs requiring transparent backgrounds
### SVG Format - Future Trend, Scalable
**Advantages:**
-   Vector graphics with infinite scaling
-   Usually smallest file size
-   Supports CSS animations and interactions
**Disadvantages:**
-   Not supported by some older browsers
-   Performance overhead for complex graphics
-   Platform compatibility issues in some cases
**Recommended Use Cases:**
-   Simple geometric designs
-   Need to adapt to multiple sizes
-   Modern browser environments
## Platform-Specific Best Practices
### Desktop Browser Optimization
```
<!-- Basic favicon configuration -->
<link rel="icon" type="image/x-icon" href="/favicon.ico">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="16x16" href="/favicon-16x16.png">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="32x32" href="/favicon-32x32.png">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="48x48" href="/favicon-48x48.png">
<!-- SVG favicon for modern browsers -->
<link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="/favicon.svg">
```
### Mobile Device Optimization
```
<!-- iOS device configuration -->
<link rel="apple-touch-icon" sizes="180x180" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">
<link rel="apple-touch-icon" sizes="152x152" href="/apple-touch-icon-152x152.png">
<!-- Android device configuration -->
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="192x192" href="/android-chrome-192x192.png">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="512x512" href="/android-chrome-512x512.png">
<!-- Mobile browser theme color -->
<meta name="theme-color" content="#000000">
```
### PWA Application Requirements
```
{
  "name": "Application Name",
  "icons": [
    {
      "src": "/android-chrome-192x192.png",
      "sizes": "192x192",
      "type": "image/png"
    },
    {
      "src": "/android-chrome-512x512.png",
      "sizes": "512x512",
      "type": "image/png"
    }
  ]
}
```
## Common Issues and Solutions
### Issue 1: Blurry Favicon Display
**Solution:**
```
<!-- Provide multiple sizes to avoid scaling -->
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="16x16" href="/favicon-16x16.png">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="32x32" href="/favicon-32x32.png">
<!-- Use SVG for vector icons -->
<link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="/favicon.svg">
```
### Issue 2: Favicon Cache Not Updating
**Solution:**
```
<!-- Add version parameter -->
<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico?v=2025010801">
```
```
// JavaScript force refresh favicon
function refreshFavicon() {
    const links = document.querySelectorAll('link[rel="icon"]');
    links.forEach(link => {
        const href = link.href;
        link.href = href + '?v=' + Date.now();
    });
}
```
## Professional Favicon Generation Tools
### Online Generation Tools (Recommended)
**1\. RealFaviconGenerator** - Enterprise-Grade Solution
-   ✅ **Comprehensive:** Generates all 15+ required sizes
-   ✅ **Platform-Specific:** Optimizes for iOS, Android, Windows
-   ✅ **PWA Support:** Includes manifest generation
-   ✅ **Quality Assurance:** Built-in testing and validation
-   🎯 **Best For:** Professional websites, e-commerce, PWAs
**2\. Favicon.io** - Quick & Creative Solution
-   ✅ **Versatile:** Text-to-icon, emoji-to-icon, image conversion
-   ✅ **Fast:** Generate complete favicon packages in seconds
-   ✅ **User-Friendly:** No technical knowledge required
-   🎯 **Best For:** Startups, personal projects, rapid prototyping
**3\. [Favicon.im](https://favicon.im/)** - Testing & Validation Tool
-   ✅ **Instant:** Quick favicon retrieval from any website
-   ✅ **Analysis:** Identify missing sizes and formats
-   ✅ **Benchmarking:** Compare your implementation with competitors
-   🎯 **Best For:** Testing, validation, competitive analysis
### Command Line Tools for Developers
```
# Professional batch generation using ImageMagick
# Source: High-quality PNG (512x512 recommended)
# Essential browser sizes
convert favicon-source.png -resize 16x16 favicon-16x16.png
convert favicon-source.png -resize 32x32 favicon-32x32.png
convert favicon-source.png -resize 48x48 favicon-48x48.png
# Mobile device sizes
convert favicon-source.png -resize 180x180 apple-touch-icon.png
convert favicon-source.png -resize 192x192 android-chrome-192x192.png
convert favicon-source.png -resize 512x512 android-chrome-512x512.png
# Optimize file sizes
optipng *.png
```
## Performance Optimization Recommendations
### File Size Control
-   Keep 16x16 and 32x32 icons under 1KB
-   Control larger sizes under 10KB
-   Use appropriate compression settings
### HTTP Caching Strategy
```
# Nginx configuration
location ~* \.(ico|png)$ {
    expires 1y;
    add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable";
}
```
## Testing and Validation
### Browser Testing Checklist
-   \[ \] Chrome (desktop and mobile versions)
-   \[ \] Firefox (desktop and mobile versions)
-   \[ \] Safari (desktop and iOS)
-   \[ \] Edge
-   \[ \] Mobile device native browsers
### Functional Testing Points
-   \[ \] Tab icons display correctly
-   \[ \] Bookmark icons are clear
-   \[ \] Mobile device "Add to Home Screen" functionality
-   \[ \] PWA installation icons
-   \[ \] Dark mode adaptation
## Complete Implementation Checklist
### Phase 1: Essential Foundation (Must-Have)
-   \[ \] **favicon.ico** - Universal browser compatibility
-   \[ \] **32x32 PNG** - High-resolution browser tabs
-   \[ \] **180x180 PNG** - iOS home screen functionality
-   \[ \] **192x192 PNG** - Android home screen compatibility
-   \[ \] **Basic HTML implementation** - Proper link tags in document head
### Phase 2: Professional Enhancement (Recommended)
-   \[ \] **16x16 PNG** - Legacy browser support
-   \[ \] **48x48 PNG** - Windows desktop shortcuts
-   \[ \] **512x512 PNG** - PWA and future-proofing
-   \[ \] **SVG favicon** - Modern browser scalability
-   \[ \] **Web manifest** - Progressive Web App support
### Phase 3: Advanced Optimization (Optional)
-   \[ \] **Theme adaptation** - Light/dark mode variants
-   \[ \] **Performance optimization** - File size compression
-   \[ \] **Caching strategy** - HTTP headers configuration
-   \[ \] **Testing validation** - Cross-platform verification
## Key Success Factors
1.  **Comprehensive Size Coverage**: Support all critical sizes from 16x16 to 512x512 pixels
2.  **Smart Format Selection**: Use ICO for compatibility, PNG for quality, SVG for scalability
3.  **Platform-Specific Optimization**: Tailor implementations for iOS, Android, Windows, and PWAs
4.  **Performance Balance**: Optimize file sizes without compromising visual quality
5.  **User Experience Focus**: Ensure consistent brand recognition across all touchpoints
6.  **Future-Proof Strategy**: Prepare for emerging display technologies and standards
## Final Recommendations
**For Enterprise/E-commerce Sites:** Use RealFaviconGenerator for comprehensive coverage, implement all critical sizes, and conduct thorough cross-platform testing.
**For Startups/Personal Projects:** Start with Favicon.io for quick setup, focus on the 4 essential sizes, and expand as your project grows.
**For Technical Teams:** Implement automated generation workflows, optimize for performance, and maintain consistent version control for all favicon assets.
By following this complete guide, you'll create a professional favicon system that enhances user experience, strengthens brand recognition, and ensures compatibility across all modern devices and platforms.

# The Complete Favicon Guide: Every Size and Format You Need in 2026 | PhotoDiet Blog
*By PhotoDiet*

[![PhotoDiet](https://www.thephotodiet.com/icon/IconText_white_noback.png)](https://www.thephotodiet.com/)

# The Complete Favicon Guide: Every Size and Format You Need in 2026
January 31, 2026 • 6 min read • by Shreeja Manandhar
Favicons have evolved far beyond the tiny 16x16 pixel icons of the early web. Modern websites need a comprehensive icon set to look professional across browsers, operating systems, and devices.
## Why Favicons Matter
A good favicon:
-   **Builds brand recognition** in browser tabs and bookmarks
-   **Improves user experience** on home screens and app launchers
-   **Signals professionalism** — missing favicons look amateurish
## The Essential Favicon Sizes
### Browser Tabs and Bookmarks
-   **16x16** — Classic favicon (still used in some browser tabs)
-   **32x32** — Standard favicon for modern browsers
-   **48x48** — Windows site pinning
### Apple Devices (iOS/macOS)
-   **180x180** — Apple Touch Icon (critical for iOS home screen)
### Android and PWA
-   **192x192** — Android Chrome home screen
-   **512x512** — PWA splash screen and manifest
### Special Cases
-   **favicon.ico** — Multi-resolution ICO file (contains 16, 32, 48px)
-   **SVG favicon** — Scalable vector for modern browsers
## How to Generate All Sizes Quickly
Creating 7+ icon sizes manually is tedious and error-prone. Our [FaviconDiet](https://www.thephotodiet.com/tools/favicon-generator/) tool generates all required sizes from a single high-resolution image:
1.  Upload your logo (ideally 512x512 or larger)
2.  Preview how it looks at each size
3.  Download a complete icon package with all sizes
## Implementation Code
Add these lines to your HTML `<head>`:
```
<link rel="icon" type="image/x-icon" href="/favicon.ico">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="32x32" href="/favicon-32x32.png">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="16x16" href="/favicon-16x16.png">
<link rel="apple-touch-icon" sizes="180x180" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">
<link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.json">
```
## Design Tips for Small Icons
-   **Simplify:** Complex logos don't work at 16px. Consider using just your logomark or initial.
-   **Test legibility:** View your favicon at actual size before finalizing.
-   **Add padding:** Icons look cramped without some breathing room.
-   **Use high contrast:** Ensure visibility on both light and dark browser themes.
## Common Mistakes to Avoid
-   **Using only favicon.ico:** This ignores Apple and PWA requirements.
-   **Low-resolution source:** Always start with at least 512x512.
-   **Transparent background issues:** Test on dark mode browsers.
## Frequently Asked Questions
### Do I really need all these sizes?
At minimum, include: 32x32 PNG, 180x180 Apple Touch Icon, and a manifest.json with 192x192 and 512x512 icons. Our generator creates the complete set automatically.
### Should I use SVG favicons?
SVG favicons are great for scalability but have limited browser support. Use them as an enhancement, not a replacement.
### How do I test my favicon?
Clear your browser cache (Ctrl+Shift+R), check in incognito mode, and test on actual mobile devices.

## Tile Sizes for Windows

Windows Tiles need 4 resolutions to work correctly: 70x70, 150x150, 310x150 and 310x310, but Microsoft recommends using 126x126, 270x270, 558x270 and 558x558 to maximize compatibility with high resolution screens.

The recommended sizes are 128x128, 270x270, 558x558 and 558x270.

## browserconfig.xml Structure

Microsoft introduced browserconfig.xml for specifying Windows tile images with a structure that includes square150x150logo and TileColor elements.

Here's a typical example structure:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<browserconfig>
  <msapplication>
    <tile>
      <square70x70logo src="/mstile-126.png"/>
      <square150x150logo src="/mstile-270.png"/>
      <wide310x150logo src="/mstile-558x270.png"/>
      <square310x310logo src="/mstile-558.png"/>
      <TileColor>#000000</TileColor>
    </tile>
  </msapplication>
</browserconfig>
```

## Important Note

The Windows Metro UI is from Windows 8, which is no longer supported by Microsoft since January 10, 2023, and the same goes for browserconfig.xml, which was related with Internet Explorer.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the `theme-color` meta tag with light and dark mode media queries:

## Key Information

The `theme-color` meta tag supports the `(prefers-color-scheme: light)` and `(prefers-color-scheme: dark)` media queries. Here's how to use it:

```html
<meta name="theme-color" content="cornflowerblue" media="(prefers-color-scheme: light)" />
<meta name="theme-color" content="dimgray" media="(prefers-color-scheme: dark)" />
```

## Browser Support

Safari 15 is the first desktop browser to support the media attribute and the prefers-color-scheme media feature on theme-color meta tags. Starting with version 93, Chrome supports it too, but only for installed progressive web apps.

## Practical Context

When toggling from light mode to dark mode on websites, the browser chrome (like the status bar on an iPhone) may not update until you refresh the page or navigate, even though the website itself changes. The `theme-color` meta tag with media queries helps keep the browser UI in sync with your page's theme.

Browsers may adjust the applied theme color based on user preferences. If a user has set a preference for light or dark mode, browsers may override the manifest theme_color value to support any prefers-color-scheme media query defined in your app's CSS.

## Universal OG Image Size

The universal size that works everywhere is 1200 × 630 pixels, satisfying requirements across Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn, Discord, Slack, and most other platforms.

## Platform-Specific Recommendations

**Facebook:**
Facebook recommends using images at 1200 x 630 pixels to display well on high-resolution devices, with a maximum file size of 8MB.

**Twitter/X:**
For Open Graph cards, Twitter uses Summary Card with Large Image at the recommended size of 1200 x 675 pixels with an aspect ratio of 1.91:1. Additionally, Twitter's 2:1 aspect ratio (1200 x 600 pixels) creates a clean, modern look that works particularly well for both desktop and mobile viewing.

**LinkedIn:**
On LinkedIn, 1200 x 1200 pixels works well for feed images and 1200 x 627 pixels for link previews.

## File Format & Size

OG images should be saved as JPEG or PNG files—JPEG is ideal for photographs, while PNG is better for images with transparency or sharper lines—and ideally kept under 5MB to ensure quick loading on social media platforms.

<!-- Source: https://blog.jim-nielsen.com/2025/dont-forget-meta-theme-color -->
# Don’t Forget the Meta Theme-Color Tag - Jim Nielsen’s Blog
*By Jim Nielsen*
---
[Jim’s Blog](https://blog.jim-nielsen.com/ "Jim Nielsen’s Blog")[](https://blog.jim-nielsen.com/subscribe/ "Subscribe")[](https://blog.jim-nielsen.com/search/ "Search")[](https://blog.jim-nielsen.com/menu/ "Menu")
# Don’t Forget the Meta Theme-Color Tag
-   2025-03-30
-   [#darkMode](https://blog.jim-nielsen.com/tags/#darkMode)
Ever used a website where you toggle from light mode to dark mode and the _web site_ changes but the chrome around the browser doesn’t?
To illustrate, take a look at this capture of my blog on an iPhone. When you toggle the theme from light to dark, note how the website turns white but status bar stays black.
![Animated gif of a dark-to-light toggle and transition on an iPhone where the entire website’s theme changes but not the status bar of the phone’s OS.](https://cdn.jim-nielsen.com/blog/2025/meta-theme-fail.gif)
Only once I refresh the page or navigate does the status bar then turn white.
When the user changes the theme on my site, I want it to propagate all the way to the surrounding context of the browser. In this case, to the status bar on the iPhone. Like this:
![Animated gif of a dark-to-light toggle and transition on an iPhone where the entire website’s theme changes including the status bar of the phone’s OS.](https://cdn.jim-nielsen.com/blog/2025/meta-theme-success.gif)
There we go! That’s what I want.
So what was wrong?
A popular way to indicate the active theme is to put a class on the root of the document, e.g.
```html
<html class="dark">
  <style>
    html { background: white }
    html.dark { background: black }
  </style>
</html>
```
Then we simply add/remove the `dark` class when the user toggles the theme.
But that will only change the in-page styles. It won’t tell the browser to update the color of whatever ambient user interface elements its drawing. For that, you’ll need [the meta theme-color tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/meta/name/theme-color):
> The `theme-color` value for the `name` attribute of the `<meta>` element indicates a suggested color that user agents should use to customize the display of the page or of the surrounding user interface.
So when you respond to the user changing their theme, don’t forget to update the `<meta name='theme-color'>` tag _in addition to_ whatever you do to modify the in-page styles.
That’ll give you the effect you want in the surrounding browser UI (for browsers that support it).
Oh, and it’s worth pointing out: [don’t forget the color-scheme property](https://blog.jim-nielsen.com/2020/color-scheme-property/) either. That’s what will tell the browser to update other in-page UI elements it draws.
So, when responding to a user preference to update a website’s theme:
1.  Toggle some global attribute that triggers style changes for all your custom, in-page elements.
2.  Set the `color-scheme` property so the browser draws the things its responsible for correctly (form controls, scroll bars, etc.).
3.  Set the `<meta name='theme-color'>` value appropriately so contextual browser UI can adapt to your site’s styles.
I wrote this post as a friendly reminder, because friends don’t let friends forget the meta theme-color tag.
---
Reply via: [Email](mailto:jimniels%2Bblog@gmail.com?subject=Re:%20blog.jim-nielsen.com/2025/dont-forget-meta-theme-color/) · [Mastodon](https://mastodon.social/@jimniels) · [Bluesky](https://bsky.app/profile/jim-nielsen.com)
```
</details></tool-use>

I've completed the web search for your query about web app manifest maskable icons, safe zones, and the "any maskable" purpose value. Here's what I found:

## Key Findings:

**Maskable Icon Purpose:** Maskable icons are designed with icon masks and safe zone in mind, such that any part of the image outside the safe zone can be ignored and masked away.

**Safe Zone Definition:** The safe zone is the area within a maskable icon which is guaranteed to always be visible, regardless of user agent preferences. It is defined as a circle with center point in the center of the icon and with a radius of 2/5 (40%) of the icon size.

**"Any Maskable" Purpose:** You can specify multiple space-separated purposes (for example, "any maskable") if you want your maskable icon to be used without a mask on other devices. However, using multiple purposes for maskable icons is not recommended, as using maskable icons as any icons adds unnecessary padding, making the core icon content smaller.

**Platform Support:** Chrome OS uses the icon with the purpose maskable, Windows uses the icon with the purpose any, macOS Sonoma or higher uses the icon with the purpose maskable, and iOS requires an extra set of icons set with apple-touch-icon and apple-touch-startup-image meta tags.

## General Browser Favicon Support (2025)

Opera will choose from any of the available icons at random, which differs from other browsers' approach to icon selection.

Only Chrome, Firefox, and Opera support SVG format favicons, though the .svg format is now the preferred choice for modern favicons, delivering crisp rendering at any zoom level or screen resolution.

For general implementation, essential favicon sizes that cover 99% of use cases include 16x16 and 32x32 pixels in ICO format for browser tabs and bookmarks, 180x180 pixels in PNG for iOS home screen and Safari bookmarks, and 192x192 pixels in PNG for Android devices and PWA requirements.
